items.q: Did all young Germans enthusiastically support the Hitler Youth?
items.correct: Many enjoyed the camps and sport, but by the war years groups such as the Edelweiss Pirates and Swing Youth openly rejected Nazi youth culture.
items.misconception: Every young German was an enthusiastic Nazi because of indoctrination.
items.random_wrong: The Hitler Youth was banned by the Nazis themselves in 1939.
items.why: Students assume propaganda worked totally; evidence of youth nonconformity is a key point about the limits of Nazi control.

items.q: Was the Gestapo watching everyone in Nazi Germany?
items.correct: The Gestapo was actually a fairly small organisation that relied heavily on denunciations volunteered by ordinary citizens.
items.misconception: The Gestapo had agents permanently stationed on every street corner.
items.random_wrong: The Gestapo was the German army's tank division.
items.why: Students picture an all-seeing police force; historians stress that popular participation through informing made the terror system work.

items.q: Did Nazi persecution of the Jews begin with death camps in 1933?
items.correct: Persecution escalated step by step — the 1933 boycott, the 1935 Nuremberg Laws, Kristallnacht in 1938, then ghettos and mass murder during the war.
items.misconception: Extermination camps were operating from the moment Hitler took power.
items.random_wrong: Persecution of Jews only ever happened outside Germany's borders.
items.why: Students telescope twelve years into one; understanding gradual escalation is central to explaining how the Holocaust became possible.

items.q: What did the Nuremberg Laws of 1935 do?
items.correct: Stripped German Jews of citizenship and banned marriage between Jews and non-Jews
items.misconception: Banned the Nazi Party from holding rallies
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Stripped German Jews of citizenship and banned marriage between Jews and non-Jews. “Banned the Nazi Party from holding rallies” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Who was the Nazi Minister of Propaganda?
items.why: It's Joseph Goebbels. “Heinrich Himmler” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What did the slogan 'Kinder, Küche, Kirche' expect of German women?
items.correct: To devote themselves to children, the home and church rather than careers
items.misconception: To serve in the armed forces
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To devote themselves to children, the home and church rather than careers. “To serve in the armed forces” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What happened on Kristallnacht in November 1938?
items.correct: Coordinated attacks destroyed synagogues and Jewish businesses, and around 30,000 Jewish men were sent to camps
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Coordinated attacks destroyed synagogues and Jewish businesses, and around 30,000 Jewish men were sent to camps. “The Berlin Olympics opened” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year were the Nuremberg Laws passed?
items.why: The real figure is 1935 AD. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
